title: Wander: The Cult of Barnacle Bay - Let’s Play and Review - Part 3
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=de7gpeHMS1Q

Part three of Daniel's (@dungeondive) **Wander: The Cult of Barnacle Bay** run opens with another rules correction — the initiative "fall back" manoeuvre costs a whole turn, not one action — which he uses to reflect on the game's tendency to include rules that quietly slow the heroes down.

With the tunnel's main tile cleared, the heroes finally get to *explore*. Ross steps onto a darkness tile and triggers the encounter deck: a giant eye peers up from a well, a knowledge check goes badly, and he's slapped by a tentacle for a wound — exactly the kind of small, flavourful non-combat beat Daniel prizes. This is his central argument for the game: the simple pairing of branching scenarios and explorable darkness tiles adds mystery and discovery that he finds sorely missing from Zombicide, Massive Darkness, and especially the "dreadful" Dark Souls board game.

Treasure flows — a thieves' cape, a heart amulet — while Tank climbs into tier two then tier three, triggering a fresh brute spawn, and a health potion (with its needlessly fractional healing rule) keeps him alive. The map cleared, the heroes activate the ladder and ascend toward the surface, and part four.

----

Daniel praises Wander for restoring exploration and mystery that he says the Dark Souls board game stripped out entirely. Which adaptation has most disappointed you by leaving out the very thing that made its source great?
